caveats: a: OEWS is NAICS 237200 rather than the prompt's 237210 label, covers all employer sizes, excludes self-employed workers, and does not isolate the narrowed service lens.
a: The LLM study measures technical exposure and potential time reduction, not realized substitution, current automation, or five-year adoption.
a: The occupation table includes property-management, sales, and amenity-related roles that may reflect business models excluded from the lens.
rho: AGC respondents are not specific to land subdivision or the lower-middle-market service subset.
rho: Use or intended investment does not establish production deployment or labor substitution.
rho: Municipality-specific rules, unstructured records, and professional accountability constrain scaling.
e: Public data do not disclose what share of NAICS 237210 receipts is fees versus lot sales or principal investment returns.
e: Some repeat developers may economically resemble operators despite taking title to land, while some nominal service firms may be captive affiliates.
e: The injected firm count is margin-bridged from receipts classes and asset-heavy developer EBITDA may not be comparable across project cycles.
s5: The source is a broad-sector modeled forecast, not an observed NAICS 237210 transfer rate.
s5: Its ten-year ownership-exit construct is wider than a five-year qualifying external control transfer.
s5: Real-estate asset transactions can look like firm transfers without transferring a recurring service operation.
q: No public source measures AI-benefit retention for the narrowed land-development service model.
q: Fee structures vary materially by client, entitlement risk, geography, and whether the operator contributes capital.
q: This estimate excludes demand, implementation cost, and asset-value effects; those must not be confused with commercial retention.
d5: Employment is only a proxy for service quantity and BLS does not publish output for this detailed industry in the table.
d5: The national housing shortage is latent demand, not a forecast that lots will be financed, approved, and developed within five years.
d5: The latest monthly housing-start comparison is volatile and subject to revision; land subdivision leads starts and varies greatly by region.
o: Neither source directly measures future self-service or software substitution in the narrowed lens.
o: Large homebuilders commonly possess internal land teams, while smaller or episodic owners may depend more on outside operators.
o: Local regulation and project complexity vary enough that national averages hide material market differences.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Development-management fees, fixed or milestone fees, reimbursed project costs, and contracted execution revenue from repeat external landowners and builders; qualifying firms coordinate approvals and physical servicing while often subcontracting field work.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 237210 is economically heterogeneous for this screen because the official definition centers on servicing and subdividing land for subsequent lot sale, which often places land and project risk in an own-account developer or special-purpose vehicle rather than an outsourced service firm. The lens is narrowed to repeat fee-based or contracted operators serving external landowners and builders so the screened population has a coherent transferable service model; the exclusion is structural, not attractiveness-based.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-account speculative developers and land inventory vehicles whose return primarily comes from land appreciation or lot sales, single-project special-purpose entities, captive builder development departments, passive landholders, non-control financing vehicles, and firms performing only legal subdivision.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing fee-based or contracted land-subdivision and development execution for external landowners, builders, or institutional clients, including entitlement coordination, development management, budgeting, subcontractor procurement, and oversight of roads, grading, drainage, and utility servicing.

a.bridge: BLS measures the NAICS 237200 occupation mix and wages, not task-level exposure or the narrowed fee-service subset. I map management, project management, finance, accounting, office support, sales, legal, drafting, and portions of construction management to the LLM paper's time-saving task construct, then discount physical construction, equipment operation, site inspection, negotiation, entitlement judgment, and tasks already automated. The full industry likely contains more own-account developer staff than the lens, so the direction of population error is uncertain; the mapping may overstate substitution where AI complements judgment and understate exposure from geospatial, document, and workflow tooling.
caveats: OEWS is NAICS 237200 rather than the prompt's 237210 label, covers all employer sizes, excludes self-employed workers, and does not isolate the narrowed service lens.
The LLM study measures technical exposure and potential time reduction, not realized substitution, current automation, or five-year adoption.
The occupation table includes property-management, sales, and amenity-related roles that may reflect business models excluded from the lens.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task-time study of qualifying fee-based land-development firms, separating already-automated work and testing AI time savings and quality for feasibility, zoning research, entitlement tracking, budgets, schedules, procurement, invoice review, meeting documentation, and client reporting.
a.rationale: Land subdivision has an unusually white-collar employment mix: management is 26.94%, business and financial operations 16.66%, office support 16.35%, and sales 5.29%, compared with 20.28% construction/extraction. High-wage construction managers, project-management specialists, accountants, financial managers, and office staff create a large wage-weighted opportunity in feasibility summaries, entitlement and meeting records, budgets, schedules, bid packages, contracts, invoices, reporting, marketing, and customer communications. The estimate remains below the economy-wide 47-56% task exposure possible with complementary LLM software because much of development value rests on site-specific judgment, negotiation, accountability, and physical execution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects land-subdivision employment from 37,100 in 2024 to 37,700 in 2034, a 1.7% ten-year increase; employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand and does not isolate the outsourced-service lens. A half-horizon translation is about 1.008, used as the base. Freddie Mac's 3.7 million-unit housing shortage supports latent need, while May 2026 housing starts 8.7% below a year earlier demonstrate cyclical downside. Productivity, service share, lot mix, rates, permitting, and regional migration can make service quantity diverge sharply from employment.
caveats: Employment is only a proxy for service quantity and BLS does not publish output for this detailed industry in the table.
The national housing shortage is latent demand, not a forecast that lots will be financed, approved, and developed within five years.
The latest monthly housing-start comparison is volatile and subject to revision; land subdivision leads starts and varies greatly by region.
d5.change_evidence: A geographic pipeline model linking entitled and proposed lots, builder lot inventories and option contracts, permit and plat applications, housing and commercial forecasts, acquisition-and-development lending, municipal utility capacity, and actual fee-service outsourcing rates.
d5.rationale: Long-run housing undersupply supports the need to entitle and service more lots, and commercial or industrial tracts add demand beyond housing. Near-term realization is highly cyclical: financing costs, mortgage affordability, builder inventories, local opposition, infrastructure capacity, and approval timelines can delay projects. BLS's nearly flat ten-year employment path justifies a base near current quantity, with a wide range to reflect a five-year development cycle.

caveats: Public data do not disclose what share of NAICS 237210 receipts is fees versus lot sales or principal investment returns.
Some repeat developers may economically resemble operators despite taking title to land, while some nominal service firms may be captive affiliates.
The injected firm count is margin-bridged from receipts classes and asset-heavy developer EBITDA may not be comparable across project cycles.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the injected LMM population using financial statements, ownership records, project entities, revenue by development fee versus lot sale, balance-sheet land inventory, external-client count, repeat engagements, and normalized EBITDA across at least three projects.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is centered on servicing and subdividing real property into lots for subsequent sale to builders; establishments may perform or subcontract the work. That population naturally contains own-account developers, land-holding entities, and project-specific vehicles whose economics are asset appreciation and lot disposition rather than repeat outsourced service. A minority should be repeat fee-based or contracted operators for external owners with transferable teams, systems, and customer relationships. The estimate therefore screens out most nominal LMM firms before considering transferability.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 237210 ($4,218,284,824) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 237210 ($16,762,013,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $731,678 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 237210); class <5: 3,459 firms, avg revenue/firm $728,217 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,966 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 554 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,286,063 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $261,571 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 251 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,262,371 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $418,885 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 163 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,560,092 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,397,783 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,657,776 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,430,359 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $190,077,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,130,176 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 212

o.bridge: The Census definition and BLS occupation mix establish a process involving land servicing, subcontracted or self-performed construction, management, finance, administration, and sales, but do not measure how much year-five quantity requires an external fee-based operator. I infer that physical servicing, entitlement accountability, procurement, budget control, stakeholder negotiation, and project risk still require an accountable operator, then discount work that large landowners or builders can self-perform and information tasks software can absorb. The proxy may overstate external-operator need because the industry's default model is often own-account, or understate it where fragmented approvals and subcontractors favor specialist coordination.
caveats: Neither source directly measures future self-service or software substitution in the narrowed lens.
Large homebuilders commonly possess internal land teams, while smaller or episodic owners may depend more on outside operators.
Local regulation and project complexity vary enough that national averages hide material market differences.
o.change_evidence: Client procurement and make-versus-buy studies from builders, institutional landowners, and municipalities, combined with vendor evidence on autonomous entitlement, feasibility, budgeting, procurement, inspection, and project-administration workflows and their effect on outsourced scope.
o.rationale: AI can automate research, document preparation, schedules, and reporting, but it cannot own entitlement representations, negotiate all site-specific conditions, finance or warrant project decisions, supervise roads and utilities, or accept completed work without accountable people. The main displacement risk is not software-only supply; it is self-service by large builders, landowners, engineering firms, or internal development teams. For the narrowed outsourced-service basket, most quantity remains operator-required, though less decisively than physical trade contracting.

caveats: No public source measures AI-benefit retention for the narrowed land-development service model.
Fee structures vary materially by client, entitlement risk, geography, and whether the operator contributes capital.
This estimate excludes demand, implementation cost, and asset-value effects; those must not be confused with commercial retention.
q.change_evidence: Executed-contract and project-P&L analysis for qualifying firms showing revenue by fixed fee, percentage fee, hourly, reimbursable cost, promote, and lot-sale participation; renewal cadence; open-book provisions; bid competition; and gross-margin persistence after documented workflow savings.
q.rationale: Within the narrowed lens, fixed and milestone development-management fees can preserve productivity gains during an engagement, while reimbursable staffing, transparent budgets, hourly professional work, and renewal negotiation pass gains to clients. Faster feasibility, approvals coordination, reporting, procurement, and invoice control may also raise project throughput, but competitive proposals and sophisticated landowner clients will rebenchmark fees over five years. A moderate majority of implemented gross benefit is therefore retained in the base case.

rho.bridge: AGC's survey covers construction firms broadly and reports AI use or investment plans, not the five-year implemented share of exposed opportunity for fee-based land-subdivision operators. I translate the 61% use-or-invest signal and specific office, estimating, and preconstruction use into a lower realized-workflow share after accounting for fragmented municipal records, GIS and ERP integration, human approvals, training, security, and client requirements. The proxy can overstate avoided labor because tool use is often assistive, but the five-year horizon and the lens's white-collar mix can make broad-construction adoption understate eventual penetration.
caveats: AGC respondents are not specific to land subdivision or the lower-middle-market service subset.
Use or intended investment does not establish production deployment or labor substitution.
Municipality-specific rules, unstructured records, and professional accountability constrain scaling.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal data from land-development software vendors and qualifying operators showing active-user penetration, workflow completion rates, hours or hires avoided, exception rates, approval accuracy, and integration costs by firm size over multiple projects.
rho.rationale: The targeted workflows are mostly digital and repeatable, making implementation more tractable than autonomous field work. Yet entitlement files span local portals and scanned documents, project facts change, outputs can affect land values and approvals, and clients require accountable professional review. A central case of one-half of exposed opportunity implemented within five years reflects meaningful office adoption without assuming end-to-end autonomous development decisions.

s5.bridge: McKinsey models nearly 124,000 ownership exits across the entire US construction sector over 2026-35, not observed five-year qualifying control sales among eligible fee-based land-subdivision firms. I shorten the horizon and discount closures, internal transitions, nontransferable owner practices, and transactions centered on project assets rather than operating-company control. This can overstate the rate because project and asset sales are common in development, or understate it if viable fee-service firms have older owners and attract strategic engineering or development buyers.
caveats: The source is a broad-sector modeled forecast, not an observed NAICS 237210 transfer rate.
Its ten-year ownership-exit construct is wider than a five-year qualifying external control transfer.
Real-estate asset transactions can look like firm transfers without transferring a recurring service operation.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transactions and sale mandates from development-company brokers, PitchBook/GF Data, land-development lenders, title records, and industry attorneys, coded to separate operating-company control sales from parcel, project, joint-venture-interest, recapitalization, family, and closure events.
s5.rationale: The broad construction succession wave supports a nontrivial transfer channel, but the eligible population is narrow and control transfer must be distinguished from selling land, individual projects, or special-purpose entities. Relationships with municipalities, builders, lenders, engineers, and contractors can be key-person dependent, which reduces the portion of owner exits that yield a transferable operating platform.

principal_driver: A white-collar-heavy workflow offers substantial AI leverage in the minority of firms that truly provide repeat external land-development services.
principal_weakness: Most nominal industry firms may be own-account developers or project vehicles rather than eligible, transferable outsourced-service operators.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as servicing land and subdividing real property into lots for later sale to builders; servicing may include excavation for roads and utilities, firms may self-perform or subcontract, and legal-only subdivision is excluded.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 237210 Land Subdivision

sources.supports: Occupation mix and wages for 34,440 jobs: management 26.94%, business/financial 16.66%, office support 16.35%, sales 5.29%, and construction/extraction 20.28%, with detailed roles including construction managers, project specialists, accountants, and laborers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 237200

sources.supports: Economy-wide task-exposure framework: about 15% of tasks directly exposed and 47-56% with complementary software, while emphasizing that technical exposure does not guarantee adoption or automation.
sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models

sources.supports: Construction adoption proxy: 61% of respondents use AI or plan increased investment, including 45% using it for office/admin, 23% for estimating, 20% for design/preconstruction, and 16% for HR-related functions.
sources.title: Dampened Expectations: 2026 Construction Hiring and Business Outlook

sources.supports: Broad succession proxy based on Census and other data: nearly 124,000 ownership exits are expected across US construction during 2026-35, and small-business owners are older than the overall population.
sources.title: The Great Ownership Transfer: A New Era of Business Stewardship

sources.supports: Projects land-subdivision employment from 37,100 in 2024 to 37,700 in 2034, an increase of 600 or 1.7%.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-34

sources.supports: Estimates the US housing shortage at 3.7 million units as of Q3 2024 and describes continued household-formation demand alongside affordability and interest-rate constraints.
sources.title: Housing Supply: Still Undersupplied by Millions of Units

sources.supports: Current cyclical indicator: May 2026 housing starts were at a 1.177 million seasonally adjusted annual rate, 8.7% below May 2025; permits were 1.413 million and 0.2% below May 2025.
sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, May 2026

title: Land Subdivision
